content.text: Arc Control Plane

Azure Arc extends the Azure Resource Manager (ARM) control plane to resources that run outside Azure — on-premises servers, Kubernetes clusters on bare metal or other clouds, and SQL Server instances on Windows or Linux. Arc does not move workloads to Azure; it creates Azure resource representations (ARM projections) of non-Azure resources so they can be managed using Azure tools: RBAC, Azure Policy, Microsoft Defender, Azure Monitor, and the Azure portal. The core mechanism is a lightweight agent (azcmagent on servers, a set of pods on Kubernetes clusters) that authenticates to Azure and registers the resource as a Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines or Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters ARM resource.

attrs.alt: Azure Arc control plane: connected machine agents on-premises create ARM projections in Azure, enabling Policy, Defender, Monitor, and Update Manager

content.text: Azure Arc does not require inbound connectivity from Azure to on-premises. The connected machine agent initiates all connections outbound on port 443 to Azure endpoints. This works through corporate proxies and firewalls that allow outbound HTTPS without inbound rules.
Arc Resource Provider Taxonomy
Resource Type
ARM Provider / Resource
Agent
Capabilities
Windows / Linux Server
Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines
azcmagent (Connected Machine Agent)
Policy, Defender, Monitor, Update Manager, SSH, Key Vault, WAC
Kubernetes Cluster
Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters
Arc Kubernetes agent pods in kube-system
GitOps (Flux), Policy, Defender, Azure Monitor, Workload Identity
SQL Server Instance
Microsoft.AzureArcData/sqlServerInstances
Azure extension for SQL (requires azcmagent)
Defender for SQL, SQL Best Practice Assessment, Microsoft Purview
Custom Locations
Microsoft.ExtendedLocation/customLocations
Enabled by Kubernetes Arc extension
Deploy Azure services (App Service, Data Services) on-premises using custom location targeting
Onboarding Servers to Arc

Onboarding a server requires running the Connected Machine Agent installer and calling azcmagent connect with credentials. For single servers, interactive device code login works. For scale (dozens or hundreds of servers), use a service principal with the Azure Connected Machine Onboarding role — this role has only the permissions needed to create the ARM resource, not to read or modify other resources. The service principal credentials are passed as environment variables or parameters to a deployment script run on each server.

# Onboard a single Linux server to Azure Arc
# Run on the server you want to connect to Azure Arc.

# Prerequisites: outbound HTTPS (443) to *.his.arc.azure.com and management.azure.com

# Option A: Interactive onboarding (one-time, generates a device code)
# Download the onboarding script from Azure Portal: Azure Arc -> Servers -> Add
wget -q https://aka.ms/azcmagent-linux -O /tmp/install_linux_azcmagent.sh
sudo bash /tmp/install_linux_azcmagent.sh

# Connect to Azure Arc with device code login (interactive)
sudo azcmagent connect \
  --resource-group 'rg-arc-servers-prod' \
  --resource-name 'server01-prod' \
  --location 'eastus' \
  --subscription-id '<subscription-id>' \
  --tenant-id '<tenant-id>'

# Option B: Service Principal onboarding (for automation / scale)
# Create a Service Principal with Azure Connected Machine Onboarding role
SP_JSON=$(az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name 'sp-arc-onboarding' \
  --role 'Azure Connected Machine Onboarding' \
  --scopes '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-arc-servers-prod')

export AZURE_CLIENT_ID=$(echo $SP_JSON | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["appId"])')
export AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET=$(echo $SP_JSON | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["password"])')
export AZURE_TENANT_ID=$(echo $SP_JSON | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["tenant"])')

# Connect using service principal
sudo azcmagent connect \
  --resource-group 'rg-arc-servers-prod' \
  --resource-name "$(hostname)" \
  --location 'eastus' \
  --subscription-id '<subscription-id>' \
  --tenant-id $AZURE_TENANT_ID \
  --service-principal-id $AZURE_CLIENT_ID \
  --service-principal-secret $AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET

# Verify connection
sudo azcmagent show
# Expected output: Status: Connected
Managing Arc-Connected Servers

After onboarding, Arc servers appear as Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines ARM resources and are manageable via az connectedmachine commands. The server's status field (Connected/Disconnected/Expired) reflects the heartbeat received from the agent. An expired server has not connected in 45+ days and requires re-onboarding. List installed extensions with az connectedmachine extension list — extensions are the primary mechanism for delivering Azure capabilities (Monitor Agent, Defender sensor, Guest Configuration) to Arc servers.

# Check and manage Arc-connected server status

RG='rg-arc-servers-prod'
SUB_ID='<subscription-id>'

# List all Arc-connected servers in a resource group
az connectedmachine list \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Status:properties.status, OS:properties.osType, AgentVer:properties.agentVersion, Location:location}' \
  -o table

# Get full details of a specific Arc server
az connectedmachine show \
  --name 'server01-prod' \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '{
    Status: properties.status,
    AgentVersion: properties.agentVersion,
    OSName: properties.osName,
    FQDN: properties.dnsFqdn,
    LastStatusChange: properties.lastStatusChange,
    AgentUpgradeable: properties.agentUpgradeStatus.upgradeValue
  }' -o json

# Arc server status values:
# Connected: agent is actively sending heartbeats (heartbeat interval: 5 min)
# Disconnected: no heartbeat received (agent stopped or network issue)
# Expired: server was disconnected for 45+ days; re-onboarding required

# List Arc server extensions installed on a server
az connectedmachine extension list \
  --machine-name 'server01-prod' \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Publisher:properties.publisher, Type:properties.type, Status:properties.provisioningState}' \
  -o table

# Upgrade the Arc agent on a server
az connectedmachine upgrade-extension \
  --machine-name 'server01-prod' \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --extension-targets '{"Microsoft.Azure.Monitor.AzureMonitorWindowsAgent":{"targetVersion":"1.22.0.0"}}'
Applying Azure Policy to Arc Servers

Azure Policy works on Arc servers via Guest Configuration — the policy engine runs inside the Arc agent's process and evaluates OS-level configuration settings (registry keys, file permissions, installed packages, service states). Guest Configuration policies require a system-assigned identity on the policy assignment and the Guest Configuration Resource Contributor role. After assignment, compliance results appear in the arc server's Azure Resource properties and in the Policy compliance dashboard alongside Azure-native resource compliance.

# Apply Azure Policy to Arc-connected servers (Guest Configuration)
# Guest Configuration policies enforce settings inside the OS via the azcmagent

# Assign built-in policy: Ensure TLS 1.2 is enabled on Linux
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'arc-linux-tls12' \
  --display-name 'Ensure TLS 1.2 or later is required on Linux Arc servers' \
  --policy 'ac8a2ef3-4a22-4e7e-b57e-2a3e3e6b74e3' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-arc-servers-prod' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

# Grant Guest Configuration resource contributor to the policy identity
POLICY_OID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name 'arc-linux-tls12' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-arc-servers-prod' \
  --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Guest Configuration Resource Contributor' \
  --assignee-object-id $POLICY_OID \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-arc-servers-prod'

# Check Guest Configuration compliance for an Arc server
az guest-configuration assignment-report list \
  --machine-name 'server01-prod' \
  --resource-group 'rg-arc-servers-prod' \
  --assignment-name 'arc-linux-tls12' \
  --subscription '<sub-id>' \
  --query '[0].properties.complianceStatus' -o tsv

# Resource Graph: all non-compliant Arc servers
az graph query -q "
  GuestConfigurationResources
  | where type =~ 'microsoft.guestconfiguration/guestconfigurationassignments'
  | where properties.complianceStatus =~ 'NonCompliant'
  | extend serverName = tostring(split(id, '/')[8])
  | project serverName, assignmentName=name, policy=tostring(properties.context.configurationInfo.name)
  | order by serverName asc
" --output table
Arc Server Health Monitoring

Five KQL queries cover the essential Arc health monitoring scenarios: servers with no heartbeat in the last 30 minutes (agent stopped or network disruption), agent version distribution across the fleet (identify upgrade candidates), Defender for Endpoint alerts from Arc servers (on-premises security posture), Windows Update compliance (missing security patches), and extension installation failures (deployment errors for Monitor Agent or Defender sensor). These queries run against the central Log Analytics workspace that the Azure Monitor Agent extension routes data to.

// KQL: Arc server health monitoring in Log Analytics

// 1. Arc servers with no heartbeat in the last 30 minutes (potentially disconnected)
Heartbeat
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where Category =~ 'Azure Monitor Agent'
| summarize LastHeartbeat = max(TimeGenerated) by Computer, SourceComputerId
| where LastHeartbeat < ago(30m)
| project Computer, LastHeartbeat, MinutesSinceHeartbeat = datetime_diff('minute', now(), LastHeartbeat)
| order by MinutesSinceHeartbeat desc

// 2. Arc agent version distribution (identify servers needing agent upgrade)
Heartbeat
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where Category =~ 'Azure Monitor Agent'
| summarize count() by ComputerEnvironment, OSType, AgentVersion=tostring(ComputerEnvironment)

// 3. Defender for Endpoint alerts from Arc servers (on-premises security events)
SecurityAlert
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| extend SubscriptionId = tostring(parse_json(ExtendedProperties).SubscriptionId)
| where AlertType startswith 'MDATP'
| summarize Count = count() by AlertName, AlertSeverity, Computer
| order by AlertSeverity asc, Count desc

// 4. Windows Update compliance for Arc servers (via Azure Update Manager)
Update
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where Classification =~ 'Security Updates' and UpdateState =~ 'Needed'
| summarize MissingSecurityUpdates = count() by Computer, SubscriptionId
| order by MissingSecurityUpdates desc

// 5. Extension installation failures on Arc servers
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where ResourceProvider =~ 'microsoft.hybridcompute'
| where OperationNameValue contains 'extensions/write'
| where ActivityStatusValue =~ 'Failed'
| project TimeGenerated, Resource, Caller=tostring(parse_json(Authorization).principalId)
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Common Arc Control Plane Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Not allowlisting Arc endpoints in the corporate firewall
azcmagent connect fails with TLS timeout errors; server never registers in Azure
Allowlist *.his.arc.azure.com, management.azure.com, *.guestconfiguration.azure.com on port 443 outbound from server subnets
Onboarding servers into the wrong subscription or resource group
Arc resources scattered across subscriptions make fleet management difficult; Policy scopes don't cover all servers
Create a dedicated 'rg-arc-servers-prod' resource group per environment; use naming conventions with location and tier
Using a service principal with more than Azure Connected Machine Onboarding role
Over-permissioned onboarding SP creates a security risk — if the SP credentials are compromised, the attacker has broad Azure access
Limit the SP to Azure Connected Machine Onboarding role scoped to the Arc server resource group
Not monitoring for Disconnected/Expired status
Expired Arc servers (45 days without heartbeat) lose all Azure management capabilities — Policy stops evaluating, Defender stops reporting, Monitor Agent stops sending data
Set up Log Analytics alert on Heartbeat table: fire when a server has no heartbeat for 60 minutes
Installing extensions before the server reaches Connected status
Extension installation fails with error 'machine is not in Connected state'
Wait for azcmagent show to return Status: Connected before installing extensions
Onboarding the same server twice (generating two ARM resources)
Duplicate ARM resources with conflicting extension deployments; compliance data split across two resource IDs
Use a consistent --resource-name (typically hostname) and check for existing ARM resource before onboarding; run azcmagent disconnect before re-onboarding
At-Scale Arc Onboarding

For environments with 50-500 servers, use a CSV-driven loop with a service principal rather than interactive device code. Generate an onboarding script template from the portal or CLI, override the resource group, tags, and credentials per-server, and push via SSH (Linux) or WinRM/PowerShell Remoting (Windows). Throttle the loop with a brief sleep between servers — Azure Resource Manager allows approximately 1,200 PUT operations per hour per subscription for the HybridCompute resource provider. After the batch completes, verify registration counts in Resource Graph before proceeding to extension deployment.

# At-scale Arc onboarding: service principal + CSV-driven loop for 50-500 servers
# The Azure Connected Machine Onboarding built-in role is sufficient.

SP_CLIENT_ID='<sp-client-id>'
SP_CLIENT_SECRET='<sp-client-secret>'
TENANT_ID='<tenant-id>'
SUBSCRIPTION_ID='<subscription-id>'
RG='arc-servers-rg'
LOCATION='eastus'

# Generate the onboarding script from the portal or CLI:
az connectedmachine generate-details \
  --location $LOCATION \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --subscription $SUBSCRIPTION_ID

# The script template (OnboardingScript.ps1) uses environment variables.
# Override per-server to land in correct RG and set tags:
cat << 'EOF' > onboard_server.ps1
$env:SUBSCRIPTION_ID = '<subscription-id>'
$env:RESOURCE_GROUP = $args[0]          # pass per-server RG as arg
$env:TENANT_ID = '<tenant-id>'
$env:LOCATION = 'eastus'
$env:AUTH_TYPE = 'principal'
$env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_ID = '<sp-client-id>'
$env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET = '<sp-client-secret>'
$env:CORRELATION_ID = [guid]::NewGuid().ToString()
$env:CLOUD = 'AzureCloud'

# Tags (key=value comma-separated)
$env:TAGS = "Environment=Production,Owner=$($args[1]),CostCenter=$($args[2])"

. { iwr -useb https://aka.ms/azcmagent-windows } | iex
& "$env:ProgramFiles\AzureConnectedMachineAgent\azcmagent.exe" connect `
  --service-principal-id $env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_ID `
  --service-principal-secret $env:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET `
  --tenant-id $env:TENANT_ID `
  --subscription-id $env:SUBSCRIPTION_ID `
  --resource-group $env:RESOURCE_GROUP `
  --location $env:LOCATION `
  --tags $env:TAGS
EOF

# Bulk onboard from inventory CSV: hostname,rg,owner,cost_center
# servers.csv example:
# webprod01,web-servers-rg,team-web,CC-001
# dbprod01,db-servers-rg,team-db,CC-002
while IFS=',' read -r hostname rg owner cost_center; do
  echo "Onboarding $hostname -> $rg"
  ssh "$hostname" 'bash -s' <<REMOTE
    # Linux inline: download + connect in one step
    export SUBSCRIPTION_ID='${SUBSCRIPTION_ID}'
    export RESOURCE_GROUP='${rg}'
    export TENANT_ID='${TENANT_ID}'
    export LOCATION='${LOCATION}'
    export AUTH_TYPE='principal'
    export SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_ID='${SP_CLIENT_ID}'
    export SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET='${SP_CLIENT_SECRET}'
    export TAGS='Environment=Production,Owner=${owner},CostCenter=${cost_center}'
    curl -L https://aka.ms/azcmagent | sudo bash
    sudo azcmagent connect \
      --service-principal-id "${SP_CLIENT_ID}" \
      --service-principal-secret "${SP_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
      --tenant-id "${TENANT_ID}" \
      --subscription-id "${SUBSCRIPTION_ID}" \
      --resource-group "${rg}" \
      --location "${LOCATION}" \
      --tags "${TAGS}"
REMOTE
  # Brief delay to avoid ARM throttling (PUT rate limit: 1200/hour per subscription)
  sleep 3
done < servers.csv

# Verify all servers registered in Resource Graph (allow 5-10 min for propagation)
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines'
   | where subscriptionId == '${SUBSCRIPTION_ID}'
   | summarize count() by tostring(properties.status)" \
  --query 'data' -o table

# Assign tags in bulk for servers missing them post-onboarding
az resource list \
  --resource-type 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines' \
  --query "[?tags.Owner==null].id" -o tsv |
while read -r id; do
  az tag update --resource-id "$id" \
    --operation Merge \
    --tags Owner=unknown CostCenter=unassigned
done
Arc VM Extensions

Arc VM extensions install into the azcmagent extension framework and expose the same extension types as native Azure VMs. Key extensions for a production Arc fleet: Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) replaces the legacy MMA/OMS agent and requires a Data Collection Rule association to ship telemetry; Microsoft Defender for Servers (MDE unified agent) requires Plan 2 on the subscription; Azure Update Manager reads patch status natively via Arc without a separate extension. Extensions are ARM resources — they appear in Resource Graph, can be assigned via policy, and can be upgraded in bulk via az connectedmachine upgrade-extension. An extension stuck in Provisioning for more than 30 minutes usually indicates the server lost internet connectivity to the Arc endpoint or the azcmagent service was stopped.

# Arc VM extensions: install monitoring, security, and management agents via ARM
# Extensions install into the azcmagent extension framework — no separate agent binary.

SERVER_ID='/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/<hostname>'

# 1. Azure Monitor Agent (replaces MMA/OMS for Arc servers)
#    Required for: VM Insights, Container Insights, Log Analytics workspace ingestion
az connectedmachine extension create \
  --name AzureMonitorLinuxAgent \
  --machine-name '<hostname>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --location eastus \
  --type AzureMonitorLinuxAgent \
  --publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor \
  --type-handler-version '1.29'

# 2. Associate with a Data Collection Rule (DCR) — required for AMA to ship data
az monitor data-collection rule association create \
  --name '<hostname>-dcr-assoc' \
  --resource "$SERVER_ID" \
  --data-collection-rule-id '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Insights/dataCollectionRules/<dcr-name>'

# 3. Microsoft Defender for Servers (Plan 2) — MDE unified agent
az connectedmachine extension create \
  --name MDE.Linux \
  --machine-name '<hostname>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --location eastus \
  --type MDE.Linux \
  --publisher Microsoft.Azure.AzureDefenderForServers \
  --type-handler-version '1.0' \
  --settings '{"azureResourceId":"'"$SERVER_ID"'"}'

# 4. Azure Update Manager (AUM) — replaces Update Management in Automation Account
#    No extension required — AUM reads from the machine directly via Arc agent.
#    Trigger an assessment and patch run:
az maintenance configuration create \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --resource-name 'arc-servers-patch-config' \
  --location eastus \
  --maintenance-scope 'InGuestPatch' \
  --install-patches-reboot-setting 'IfRequired' \
  --install-patches-linux-parameters-package-name-masks-to-include '[]' \
  --install-patches-linux-parameters-package-name-masks-to-exclude '[]' \
  --install-patches-linux-parameters-classifications-to-include '["Critical","Security"]'

az maintenance assignment create \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --resource-name '<hostname>' \
  --provider-name 'Microsoft.HybridCompute' \
  --resource-type 'machines' \
  --maintenance-configuration-id '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Maintenance/maintenanceConfigurations/arc-servers-patch-config' \
  --name 'arc-patch-assignment'

# 5. List all extensions and their status across Arc fleet
az connectedmachine extension list \
  --machine-name '<hostname>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Type:type, Version:typeHandlerVersion, Status:provisioningState}' \
  -o table

# 6. Upgrade all extensions to latest versions (safe idempotent operation)
az connectedmachine upgrade-extension \
  --machine-name '<hostname>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --extension-targets '{
    "Microsoft.Azure.Monitor.AzureMonitorLinuxAgent":{"targetVersion":"1.29"},
    "Microsoft.Azure.AzureDefenderForServers.MDE.Linux":{"targetVersion":"1.0"}
  }'

# Resource Graph: find servers missing AzureMonitorLinuxAgent extension
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines'
   | mv-expand extensions = properties.extensions
   | summarize exts = make_set(extensions.name) by name, resourceGroup
   | where not(exts has 'AzureMonitorLinuxAgent')" \
  --query 'data' -o table
Summary

Azure Arc extends Azure Resource Manager to non-Azure resources by installing lightweight agents (azcmagent on servers, Arc agent pods on Kubernetes) that register the resource as an ARM projection in the Microsoft.HybridCompute or Microsoft.Kubernetes provider. This projection allows Azure RBAC, Policy, Defender, Monitor Agent, and Update Manager to manage on-premises and multi-cloud resources using the same tools as Azure-native resources. Onboard at scale using a service principal limited to the Azure Connected Machine Onboarding role. Monitor heartbeat status to detect disconnected servers before they expire (45 days without heartbeat requires re-onboarding).
